figures.name: Mount Sumeru
figures.gloss: The golden world-mountain at the centre of every world-system, ringed by seven golden ranges and seven seas, with the heavens stacked at its summit and the hells beneath its root — the axle around which the cosmos is built.
figures.facet: The same mountain under a new law: Buddhism inherited Meru entire from the Vedic world, then hollowed out its theism — Sumeru stands at the centre, but no creator sits on its peak, only Mara's heaven of desire and the impermanent gods of Indra's host.
figures.provenance: Systematised in the Abhidharmakośa (Vasubandhu, c. 4th–5th c. CE) and the Pali Abhidhamma; the geography is shared Indic inheritance from the older Meru cosmology.

figures.name: Māra
figures.gloss: The lord of the desire-realm, death and delusion who assails the Buddha beneath the Bodhi tree with armies, temptresses and the demand 'by what right do you sit there?' — the one who would keep all beings bound to the wheel.
figures.facet: An adversary disarmed, not defeated: Māra is not slain like a chaos-dragon but simply seen-through — the Buddha touches the earth to witness him, and his armies dissolve. Evil here is a misreading of reality, not a rival kingdom.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Padhāna Sutta, Māra-saṃyutta) and later hagiography of the Bodhi-tree assault.

figures.name: Yama (Enma)
figures.gloss: Judge and king of the dead, who holds up the mirror of karma in which each soul sees its own deeds and is sent to its rebirth — inherited from the Vedic first-mortal and carried east into Chinese and Japanese hells as Yanluo and Enma.
figures.facet: A judge who only shows: Yama passes no arbitrary sentence — the karmic mirror reflects what the being itself has done, so judgement is exposure, not decree. The cosmic-order, not the king, condemns.
figures.provenance: Directly continuous with Vedic Yama; the East-Asian Ten Kings of Hell elaborate him through Tang-era apocrypha.

figures.name: Avalokiteśvara
figures.gloss: The bodhisattva of boundless compassion, who hears every cry of the world and takes whatever form will save the one who calls — eleven heads, a thousand arms, and across the seas becoming Guanyin, Kannon, Chenrezig.
figures.facet: One mercy, ten thousand faces: where another tradition would split this into a pantheon, Buddhism insists the multiplicity is a single compassionate nature meeting each being in its own shape — the one-through-many made into a doctrine of skilful means.
figures.provenance: Lotus Sūtra (Universal Gateway chapter) and the Kāraṇḍavyūha; the gender shift to the female Guanyin occurs in Chinese transmission from the Tang onward.

figures.name: The Three Bodies (Trikāya)
figures.gloss: The doctrine that every Buddha is one awakened reality in three bodies — the formless truth-body (dharmakāya), the radiant heaven-body (saṃbhogakāya), and the historical man who walked and taught (nirmāṇakāya).
figures.facet: The unifying key of the whole shard: countless buddhas and bodhisattvas are not many gods but one awakened nature refracted — the trikāya is the explicit theology of the one-through-many, naming how the absolute appears as the manifold.

figures.name: The Kalpas
figures.gloss: The immense aeons of cosmic time, each world-system arising, enduring, dissolving in fire, water and wind, and lying void before it forms again — measured by similes of mountains worn away by a passing cloth once a century.
figures.facet: Destruction without a destroyer: the worlds dissolve and reform by impersonal law, not by a god's will or wrath — cyclic time as the breathing of dependent-origination itself, with no hand to start or stop it.

figures.name: The Wheel of Becoming (Bhavacakra)
figures.gloss: The six-spoked wheel of the realms — gods, titans, humans, beasts, hungry ghosts, hells — turned by the three poisons at its hub and clutched in the jaws of Yama, the whole round of rebirth made into a single image.
figures.facet: A cosmos drawn as a diagnosis: the wheel is not a map to revere but a prison to escape, every realm including the heavens marked as impermanent suffering — the great-cycle reframed as the very thing salvation undoes.

figures.name: Anātman (No-Self)
figures.gloss: The teaching that there is no eternal self and no creator-ground — only a stream of conditioned arising, empty of any fixed essence, where the Vedic schools found the deathless Ātman one with Brahman.
figures.facet: The precious divergence — the shard that says NO: against the Upaniṣadic Brahman-Ātman and against every withdrawn-creator, Buddhism denies both the world-soul and the maker, putting impersonal law (dharma, dependent-origination) where other traditions put a god. Its 'cosmic-order' is order all the way down, with nothing behind it.
figures.provenance: Pali Canon (Anattalakkhaṇa Sutta) and Madhyamaka emptiness (Nāgārjuna). Stated here as an explicit, honoured contradiction of Brahman/Ātman — not a parallel.

figures.name: Śakra (Indra)
figures.gloss: The Vedic thunder-king demoted to a Buddhist householder-god — lord of the Heaven of the Thirty-Three on Sumeru's summit, still wielding the thunderbolt, but now a pious if impermanent disciple subject to rebirth.
figures.facet: The sky-king as a cautionary tale: Śakra keeps Indra's vajra and his heaven, yet Buddhism makes him mortal and unenlightened — a once-supreme god enrolled as a student, the storm-wielder humbled beneath the law he too must obey.

edges.note: Sumeru IS Meru — the same world-mountain, the same Sanskrit name (su-meru, 'excellent Meru'), carried from Vedic into Buddhist cosmology as direct Indic inheritance. The brightest seam in the shard.
The quartered cosmos around a central mountain — joint membership, shared with Yggdrasil, the Ceiba, Eden's rivers and Meru itself.
One and the same deity by descent: Vedic Yama, first-mortal and king of the dead, inherited wholesale into Buddhism and carried east as Yanluo / Enma. Indic-internal cognate.
Śakra is Indra — the Vedic storm-king himself, name and vajra retained, but reduced to an impermanent heaven-god subject to rebirth. Direct inheritance, then demotion.

The load-bearing divergence: anātman is the explicit DENIAL of the Brahman/Ātman of the Upaniṣads — same Indic conversation, opposite verdict. No eternal self, no world-soul. Tiered 1 because the contradiction is a documented historical argument within one tradition, not a loose parallel.
Against the Sāṃkhya eternal Puruṣa (the witness-self), Buddhism denies any abiding witness — a documented doctrinal dispute between Indian schools.

Within the shard Māra and Yama are twin faces of death — Māra-as-death the tempter who keeps beings bound, Yama-as-death the judge who sorts them; in some texts Māra is even called the lord over the realms Yama rules.
Māra (or Yama) clasps the Wheel of Becoming in his jaws — the adversary IS the round of rebirth that the Buddha steps out of.
Avalokiteśvara's thousand forms are the trikāya doctrine in practice — one awakened nature taking every shape; the principle and its richest example, both Mahāyāna.
Two scales of the one cyclic vision — the kalpas the great breathing of world-systems, the wheel the turning of individual rebirth; both impersonal, both within the shard.

Speculative: the Bodhi-tree temptation rhymes with the wilderness temptation of Christ by Satan — the tempter who tests the awakener with power and doubt. A structural rhyme of motif, no contact; flagged.
The temptation-and-victory pattern: a saviour assailed by the prince of this world and overcoming through refusal — Bodhi tree and wilderness as the same narrative joint, independently arrived at.
Judge-of-the-dead who weighs deeds: Yama's karmic mirror and Anubis's scale both make the verdict an exposure of what the soul itself carries — order judging, not whim.
Sovereign of the land of the dead through which all the unliberated must pass — a structural parallel of the underworld-king, no contact.
King and judge of the dead presiding over the reckoning — analogous office, separate families.
Two psychopomps within the shard at opposite poles — Avalokiteśvara the mercy that guides souls out of the hells, Yama the law that assigns them; compassion and judgement on the same wheel.
The compassionate intercessor who hears the suffering and saves — Guanyin and Isis as the merciful divine mother answering the cry; functional parallel across families.

Both name the one reality appearing as the many — yet they diverge at the root: Brahman is the eternal Self the trikāya's dharmakāya pointedly is NOT. Kinship in structure, documented opposition in content.
One sacred reality refracted into countless masks (ixiptla / nirmāṇakāya) — the one-through-many reached independently in Mesoamerica and in Mahāyāna.
The formless absolute (dharmakāya / Ein Sof) that emanates the manifest worlds — a structural parallel of the unmanifest ground, no contact.
Impersonal cosmic process unfolding by its own law without a willing creator — the kalpas and the Dao both put order in the place where other traditions put a god.
Both name an order with no personal maker behind it — cosmic-order as the floor of reality; a true functional convergence, distinct families.
Time as successive worlds destroyed and remade — the Mesoamerican Five Suns and the Buddhist kalpas both read history as recurring catastrophe-and-renewal, independently.

The same god, inverted in rank: Vedic Indra is the supreme storm-king; Buddhist Śakra is the same figure demoted to an impermanent, unenlightened disciple — a documented theological re-ranking within Indic religion.
The thunderbolt-wielding sky-king atop the world-mountain's heaven — Śakra and Zeus as the storm-sovereign archetype, with Buddhism uniquely subordinating him to a higher law.
Speculative: the lord of delusion and the smoking-mirror lord of fate and temptation both rule the realm of binding desire — a reach of theme, no descent; flagged.
The world-axis with heavens above and hells below — Sumeru and the world-tree as the same cosmological spine, separate families.
